narrative_summary.historical_use: Known in Traditional Chinese Medicine for over 2,000 years as 'Lingzhi' (spirit mushroom), revered as the 'mushroom of immortality'. Used by Taoist monks to promote longevity, calm the mind, and nourish the heart. Referenced in the Shen Nong Ben Cao Jing (c. 100 CE) as a superior tonic.
narrative_summary.modern_application: Clinically studied as an adjunct in oncology for immunomodulation — trials show increased NK cell and T-lymphocyte activity. Used for hepatoprotection, blood pressure reduction, and anti-fatigue. A 2016 Cochrane review found benefit in cancer patients for immune response but insufficient evidence to recommend as primary cancer therapy.
narrative_summary.side_effects: GI upset (nausea, diarrhoea), dry mouth, dizziness, and skin rash with prolonged use (>3–6 months). Rare but documented cases of hepatotoxicity, predominantly associated with powdered (not extract) preparations. Potential for increased bleeding time.
narrative_summary.contraindications: Contraindicated with anticoagulants (warfarin, heparin) and antiplatelet drugs due to platelet aggregation inhibition. Use with caution alongside immunosuppressants and chemotherapy agents. Avoid prior to surgery.

clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: Immunomodulation via activation of NK cells, macrophages, and dendritic cells through TLR-4/NF-κB signalling. Ganoderic acids inhibit HMG-CoA reductase (cholesterol synthesis), suppress NF-κB transcription, and induce tumour cell apoptosis via the intrinsic mitochondrial pathway. Antioxidant activity via Nrf2/HO-1 upregulation.

pharmacokinetics.absorption: Polysaccharides show low oral bioavailability; ganoderic acids are absorbed but undergo significant first-pass metabolism. Bioavailability data from human PK studies is limited.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: Ganoderic acids are highly lipophilic and distribute preferentially into the liver, adipose tissue, and kidneys. Plasma protein binding of triterpenoids exceeds 90%. Polysaccharide fractions concentrate at gut-associated lymphoid tissue (GALT) and spleen. No quantitative human tissue distribution data are available.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: Hepatic; ganoderic acids are substrates of CYP3A4. Triterpenoids undergo glucuronidation and sulfation.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: Primarily biliary (feces) for polysaccharides; renal excretion for water-soluble metabolites.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: Anticoagulants (warfarin, aspirin, clopidogrel) — potentiates antiplatelet effects. Antihypertensives — additive hypotensive effect. Immunosuppressants (cyclosporine, tacrolimus) — potential antagonism. CYP3A4 substrates — possible competitive inhibition.
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: LD50 >5 g/kg in mice (oral). Chronic use at high doses associated with rare hepatotoxicity (elevated ALT/AST); reversible upon discontinuation.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Insufficient human safety data. Animal studies show no teratogenicity at low doses, but high-dose use is not recommended. Avoid during first trimester.
special_precautions.lactation: Excretion in human breast milk unknown. Avoid due to insufficient safety data.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: Use with caution. Rare hepatotoxicity reported; baseline and periodic liver enzyme monitoring recommended in patients with pre-existing hepatic disease.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: Limited specific data. No established contraindication at standard doses; dose reduction may be prudent in severe renal impairment.

narrative_summary.historical_use: Used in Traditional Chinese and Japanese medicine for centuries primarily to treat gastrointestinal disorders (gastric ulcers, chronic gastritis) and as a general tonic. Known in Japan as 'Yamabushitake'. Referenced in historical texts as a food and medicine for digestive health.
narrative_summary.modern_application: Clinically studied for its nerve growth factor (NGF)-stimulating properties. A 2009 double-blind RCT (Mori et al.) demonstrated significant improvement in mild cognitive impairment after 16 weeks. Evidence supports benefits in depression, anxiety, and peripheral nerve regeneration. Investigated as a neuroprotective agent in Alzheimer's and Parkinson's disease models.
narrative_summary.side_effects: Generally well-tolerated. Reported adverse effects include mild GI discomfort (nausea, bloating), and rare allergic reactions including contact dermatitis and respiratory allergy in sensitised individuals.
narrative_summary.contraindications: Known allergy to Hericium species or other mushrooms. Caution with anticoagulants due to potential platelet inhibition. Caution in autoimmune conditions given immunomodulatory properties.

clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: Hericenones and erinacines stimulate de novo synthesis and secretion of NGF (nerve growth factor) in astrocytes, promoting neuronal survival and axonal growth. Erinacines cross the blood-brain barrier (demonstrated in rodent studies) via passive diffusion. Anti-neuroinflammatory effects via inhibition of MAPK and NF-κB pathways. Promotes hippocampal neurogenesis via TrkA/Erk1/2 signalling.

pharmacokinetics.absorption: Erinacine A crosses the blood-brain barrier in rodents; human CNS penetration not directly measured. Hericenone bioavailability in humans is not well characterised.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: Erinacine A distributes into CNS tissue in rodent models, including the hippocampus and cortex. Hericenones are lipophilic and expected to distribute into lipid-rich tissues. No human plasma protein binding or tissue distribution data are available.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: Limited human data. Hepatic phase I/II metabolism assumed based on compound structure.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: Limited human data. Presumed renal and biliary excretion of metabolites.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: Anticoagulants (theoretical antiplatelet effect). Antidiabetic agents — may potentiate hypoglycaemia. No significant CYP450 interactions established in human studies.
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: LD50 >5 g/kg (oral, mice). No organ toxicity identified in 28-day subacute studies in rodents. Human clinical trials up to 3 g/day for 16 weeks showed no significant adverse effects.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Insufficient human clinical data. No animal teratogenicity studies identified. Avoid due to lack of safety evidence.
special_precautions.lactation: Excretion in human breast milk unknown. Avoid during breastfeeding.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: No hepatotoxicity reported in literature. No dose adjustment studies available; use with standard monitoring.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: No specific contraindication identified. Limited data in renal impairment populations.

narrative_summary.historical_use: Used in folk medicine across Russia, Siberia, and Scandinavia (including Norway and Finland) since at least the 16th century. Traditionally prepared as a tea (decoction of the sclerotium) for GI diseases, tuberculosis, and cancer. Finnish records describe its use as a coffee substitute during WWII.
narrative_summary.modern_application: Studied primarily for antioxidant and anti-tumour activity. Betulinic acid and polysaccharides show anti-proliferative effects in vitro and in animal models. Immunomodulatory properties documented. Anti-inflammatory and anti-hyperglycaemic effects seen in preclinical studies. Human RCT data remains limited.
narrative_summary.side_effects: Oxalate nephropathy with excessive long-term consumption (case reports documented). Potential hypoglycaemia. Rare hypersensitivity reactions. Theoretical bleeding risk.
narrative_summary.contraindications: Contraindicated in patients with chronic kidney disease or urolithiasis due to very high oxalate content. Avoid with anticoagulants and antidiabetic medications without monitoring. Avoid before surgery.

clinical_data.used_part: Sclerotium (sterile conk growing on birch trees)
clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: Betulinic acid induces tumour cell apoptosis via the mitochondrial pathway (cytochrome c release, caspase-3 activation). Beta-glucans activate macrophages and NK cells via Dectin-1 and TLR-2/4 receptors. Melanin-glucan complex acts as a potent free-radical scavenger (DPPH, ABTS assays). Anti-inflammatory activity via inhibition of COX-2 and iNOS expression.

pharmacokinetics.absorption: Betulinic acid has very poor oral bioavailability (<5%) due to low aqueous solubility. Polysaccharides have limited systemic absorption; exert local GI immunomodulatory effects.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: Betulinic acid is highly lipophilic (logP ≈ 7.8) and distributes extensively into adipose tissue and the liver; plasma protein binding exceeds 99%. It does not readily penetrate the blood-brain barrier due to poor aqueous solubility. Polysaccharide fractions remain largely in the GI tract and GALT. No quantitative human tissue distribution data are available.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: Betulinic acid undergoes hepatic oxidative metabolism. Detailed human PK data not available.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: Fecal excretion of unabsorbed polysaccharides and betulinic acid; oxalate excreted renally.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: Anticoagulants (warfarin, aspirin) — potential additive antiplatelet effect. Antidiabetic agents (insulin, metformin) — additive hypoglycaemic risk. No established CYP450 drug interactions in humans.
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: LD50 data not established for whole extract in humans. High-dose aqueous extract associated with oxalate nephropathy (case reports: PMID 26392405). Renal accumulation of oxalate is the primary toxicity concern with chronic high-dose use.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Insufficient safety data. Traditionally contraindicated. Avoid during pregnancy.
special_precautions.lactation: No data on excretion in breast milk. Avoid during breastfeeding.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: No specific hepatotoxicity identified. Limited data; standard caution applies.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: Contraindicated or use with extreme caution due to high oxalate content — documented cases of oxalate nephropathy even in previously healthy individuals with excessive intake.

narrative_summary.historical_use: Documented in Tibetan and Chinese medicine since at least the 15th century (Ben Cao Bei Yao, 1694). Traditionally collected at high altitude on the Tibetan Plateau, growing from the larvae of Hepialus moth caterpillars. Used to treat fatigue, respiratory weakness, sexual dysfunction, and as a general tonic for the elderly.
narrative_summary.modern_application: Studied for anti-fatigue and athletic performance enhancement (increased VO2 max in elderly subjects). Clinically trialled for chronic kidney disease — several Chinese RCTs show reduction in proteinuria and creatinine. Immunomodulatory and anti-tumour properties documented in preclinical models. Expensive wild form largely replaced by cultured mycelium (CS-4 strain).
narrative_summary.side_effects: Mild GI upset (nausea, diarrhoea, dry mouth). Rare hypersensitivity reactions. Risk of hypoglycaemia with antidiabetic co-medication. Risk of bleeding with anticoagulants.
narrative_summary.contraindications: Anticoagulant therapy. Immunosuppressive therapy (cyclosporine — may potentiate nephrotoxicity or immunosuppression). Autoimmune diseases — use with caution. Not recommended pre-surgery.

clinical_data.used_part: Fruiting body (stromata) and mycelium; cultured mycelium (CS-4) used commercially
clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: Cordycepin inhibits mRNA polyadenylation, blocking RNA processing and thereby suppressing inflammatory cytokine production and tumour cell proliferation. Adenosine and cordycepin act on adenosine receptors (A1, A2A, A3), modulating cardiovascular and immune tone. Increases cellular ATP production via AMPK-mediated mitochondrial biogenesis. Anti-inflammatory via suppression of NF-κB and COX-2.

pharmacokinetics.absorption: Cordycepin is rapidly deaminated in plasma by adenosine deaminase to the less active 3'-deoxyinosine, significantly limiting systemic bioavailability. Plasma half-life is very short (minutes) unless adenosine deaminase is inhibited.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: Cordycepin distributes rapidly to tissues via equilibrative nucleoside transporters (ENTs) but is simultaneously deaminated in plasma. In animal models, intact cordycepin has been detected in liver, kidney, and spleen. Adenosine distributes widely to all tissues. No quantitative human tissue distribution studies are available for Cordyceps sinensis preparations.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: Rapid deamination by adenosine deaminase; hepatic xanthine oxidase pathway for purine catabolism.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: Renal excretion of deaminated metabolites.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: Cyclosporine — potential pharmacodynamic interaction (immunosuppression, nephrotoxicity). Anticoagulants — additive antiplatelet effects. Antidiabetic drugs — additive hypoglycaemia risk. May interact with theophylline via adenosine receptor competition.
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: LD50 >3 g/kg (oral, mice) for cultured mycelium extract. Generally well-tolerated in clinical trials up to 3–4.5 g/day. No significant organ toxicity at standard doses.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Insufficient clinical safety data. Traditionally used as a uterine stimulant in some contexts — avoid during pregnancy.
special_precautions.lactation: No data on transfer to breast milk. Avoid during breastfeeding.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: No documented hepatotoxicity. Limited data in hepatic impairment; standard caution recommended.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: Paradoxically studied as a renoprotective agent in CKD. Some RCTs show benefit in reducing proteinuria; however, monitoring of renal function is advised and dose adjustment may be needed in severe impairment.

narrative_summary.historical_use: Historically used interchangeably with C. sinensis in Traditional Chinese Medicine, though less prized. Growing interest as a cultivatable alternative since wild C. sinensis is prohibitively expensive and endangered. Used in TCM for similar indications: fatigue, sexual dysfunction, respiratory conditions.
narrative_summary.modern_application: Contains higher concentrations of cordycepin than wild C. sinensis and is more accessible for research. A double-blind RCT (Hirsch et al., 2017) demonstrated improved VO2 max and time-to-exhaustion in active adults. Anti-inflammatory effects documented both in vitro and in vivo. Investigated for anti-tumour, antidiabetic, and renoprotective activities.
narrative_summary.side_effects: Mild GI symptoms (nausea, diarrhoea). Rare allergic reactions. Potential hypoglycaemia with concurrent antidiabetic use.
narrative_summary.contraindications: Anticoagulant therapy. Autoimmune conditions — theoretical risk of exacerbation. Avoid pre-surgery. Caution with immunosuppressants.

primary_active_compounds: Cordycepin (3'-deoxyadenosine — higher concentration than C. sinensis)
clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: Cordycepin is the primary bioactive; inhibits mRNA polyadenylation, reducing expression of pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1β). Activates AMPK pathway, enhancing mitochondrial biogenesis. Immunomodulation via NK cell and macrophage activation. Anti-tumour activity via cell cycle arrest at G2/M phase and apoptosis induction.

pharmacokinetics.absorption: Cordycepin is rapidly deaminated by adenosine deaminase after oral administration; concurrent administration of adenosine deaminase inhibitors (e.g., pentostatin) dramatically increases bioavailability. Human PK studies are limited.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: Cordycepin distributes broadly via equilibrative nucleoside transporters (ENTs) to muscle, liver, and kidney. In animal models, intact cordycepin has been detected in tumour tissue. Rapid plasma deamination limits systemic exposure of the intact molecule. No quantitative human tissue distribution studies are available.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: Adenosine deaminase-mediated deamination is the primary metabolic step; subsequent hepatic purine catabolism.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: Renal excretion of polar metabolites.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: Anticoagulants — additive platelet inhibition. Antidiabetic drugs — additive hypoglycaemia risk. Immunosuppressants — pharmacodynamic interaction. Adenosine deaminase inhibitors (pentostatin, deoxycoformycin) — markedly increase cordycepin exposure.
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: LD50 >3 g/kg (oral, rodents). In 4-week subacute toxicity studies in rodents, no significant organ toxicity observed. Clinical trial data (3 g/day for 3 weeks) showed no significant adverse effects.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Insufficient clinical data. Avoid during pregnancy based on lack of safety evidence.
special_precautions.lactation: No data available on excretion in human breast milk. Avoid during breastfeeding.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: No hepatotoxicity reported in literature. Limited specific data; standard monitoring recommended.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: Preclinical evidence suggests renoprotective potential. No dose adjustment guidelines available; monitor renal function with regular use.

narrative_summary.historical_use: Used in Traditional Chinese Medicine as 'Yun Zhi' (cloud mushroom) for over 2,000 years for liver ailments, infections, and as a general tonic. In Japan, the polysaccharide extract PSK (Krestin) was developed in the 1970s and became one of the best-selling anti-cancer drugs in the world, approved for clinical use in Japan.
narrative_summary.modern_application: PSK (polysaccharide-K) and PSP (polysaccharide-peptide) are the most clinically validated compounds. Meta-analyses of Japanese RCTs show improved 5-year survival in gastric and colorectal cancer when PSK is used as adjunct to chemotherapy. US FDA granted IND approval for a clinical trial in breast cancer. Also shown to modulate gut microbiome composition beneficially.
narrative_summary.side_effects: Generally very well tolerated. Mild GI symptoms (nausea, diarrhoea) at high doses. Rare hypersensitivity. Darkening of fingernails reported with PSK in some trials. Mild hepatotoxicity at very high doses in animal studies.
narrative_summary.contraindications: Caution with immunosuppressants (pharmacodynamic antagonism). Avoid in known mushroom allergy. Caution pre-surgery.

clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: PSK and PSP bind to TLR-2 and Dectin-1 on macrophages and dendritic cells, activating innate immunity. Stimulates NK cell cytotoxicity and T-helper cell polarisation. PSK upregulates IL-2, IFN-γ production. Antioxidant via direct radical scavenging. Prebiotic effect on Bifidobacterium and Lactobacillus populations in the gut.

pharmacokinetics.absorption: PSK is a high-molecular-weight glycoprotein with limited systemic oral absorption; primarily acts at gut-associated lymphoid tissue (GALT) and is partially absorbed in lower molecular weight fractions.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: PSK distributes primarily to GALT, spleen, and peripheral lymphoid organs after partial oral absorption. Animal studies demonstrate accumulation in lymphoid tissue and tumour microenvironment. High molecular weight limits CNS penetration. No human plasma protein binding or quantitative tissue distribution data are available.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: Partially hydrolysed in the GI tract; absorbed fractions undergo hepatic metabolism.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: Primarily fecal; some renal excretion of smaller absorbed fragments.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: Chemotherapy agents (cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin) — no significant pharmacokinetic interaction; may be administered concurrently. Immunosuppressants — pharmacodynamic antagonism. Warfarin — theoretical interaction; monitor INR.
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: PSK LD50 >20 g/kg (oral, mice). In Japanese post-marketing surveillance of millions of doses, serious adverse events are extremely rare. Mild, reversible liver enzyme elevations at doses >6 g/day in some individuals.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Insufficient human clinical data. Avoid during pregnancy due to lack of controlled safety studies.
special_precautions.lactation: No data on excretion in breast milk. Avoid during breastfeeding.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: Mild ALT/AST elevations noted at high doses; use cautiously with hepatic impairment and monitor liver function.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: No specific dose adjustment data available. Use with standard monitoring.

narrative_summary.historical_use: Cultivated and used as food and medicine in China and Japan for over 1,000 years. Referenced in Chinese medical texts as early as 100 CE (Liu Ming Ben Cao). Traditionally used for improving vitality, treating colds, liver conditions, and as an aphrodisiac. The most widely cultivated edible mushroom globally.
narrative_summary.modern_application: Lentinan, the primary beta-glucan, is approved in Japan as an adjunct in gastric cancer treatment (IV formulation). Oral AHCC (Active Hexose Correlated Compound), derived from shiitake mycelium, is widely studied for immune enhancement. Anti-hypercholesterolaemic properties supported by multiple RCTs. Eritadenine shown to reduce serum LDL. Antiviral properties investigated (including HPV clearance in a small RCT).
narrative_summary.side_effects: Shiitake dermatitis (flagellate erythema) — a distinctive linear skin rash triggered by consumption of raw or undercooked mushroom (due to lentinan). GI upset. Eosinophilia reported. Rare hypersensitivity pneumonitis with occupational exposure (spore inhalation).
narrative_summary.contraindications: Known shiitake allergy (dermatitis risk). Avoid concurrent use with immunosuppressants. Caution with anticoagulants.

clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: Lentinan activates macrophages, NK cells, and T-cells via Dectin-1 and complement receptor 3 (CR3). Upregulates IL-12, TNF-α, IFN-γ. Eritadenine competitively inhibits S-adenosylhomocysteine hydrolase, lowering plasma homocysteine and LDL-cholesterol. AHCC activates NK and dendritic cells and modulates alpha-glucan-dependent pathways.

pharmacokinetics.absorption: Lentinan is poorly absorbed orally (hence IV formulation in clinical use). AHCC alpha-glucan fractions have better oral bioavailability due to smaller molecular weight. Eritadenine is absorbed orally.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: Lentinan (IV) distributes to lymphoid tissue, tumour stroma, and peritoneal cavity; does not cross the blood-brain barrier. AHCC fractions distribute to lymphoid tissue and liver. Eritadenine distributes systemically. No human plasma protein binding data are available.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: Eritadenine undergoes hepatic metabolism. Lentinan is degraded by intestinal microbiota and digestive enzymes.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: Renal excretion of eritadenine metabolites; fecal for unabsorbed polysaccharides.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: Immunosuppressants — pharmacodynamic antagonism. Anticoagulants — monitor; theoretical platelet inhibition. Chemotherapy — lentinan used as adjunct without significant PK interaction in Japanese studies.
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: Lentinan IV: well-tolerated; eosinophilia reported in some trials. Oral whole mushroom generally very safe at dietary quantities. Dermatitis risk from raw mushroom consumption is the most clinically relevant adverse effect.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Dietary consumption considered safe. Medicinal doses (concentrated extracts) — insufficient data; avoid.
special_precautions.lactation: Dietary quantities considered acceptable. Concentrated supplemental doses — insufficient data.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: No hepatotoxicity documented. AHCC has been studied in hepatitis C — generally safe. Standard monitoring recommended with concentrated preparations.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: No specific risk identified. No dose adjustment data available.

narrative_summary.historical_use: Used in Traditional Chinese and Japanese medicine as a tonic and food. Known in Japan as 'Maitake' (dancing mushroom), legend holds that people danced with joy upon finding it in the wild. Used historically for fatigue, diabetes-like symptoms, and immune support. Cultivated commercially since the 1970s in Japan.
narrative_summary.modern_application: D-fraction (MD-fraction), a specific beta-1,6/1,3-glucan with protein, has shown immunomodulatory and anti-tumour effects in preclinical and small human trials. A 2009 non-randomised study in breast cancer patients showed immune stimulation. SX-fraction (a specific polysaccharide fraction) has demonstrated antidiabetic effects in humans, reducing insulin resistance and blood glucose. Antihypertensive and lipid-lowering effects also documented.
narrative_summary.side_effects: Generally well tolerated. Mild GI symptoms. Risk of hypoglycaemia with antidiabetic medications. Theoretical bleeding risk.
narrative_summary.contraindications: Antidiabetic medications — risk of additive hypoglycaemia. Anticoagulants — theoretical risk. Immunosuppressants — pharmacodynamic antagonism.

clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: D-fraction binds Dectin-1 and TLR-2 on macrophages and dendritic cells, activating innate and adaptive immune responses; increases NK cell and CD4+ T-cell activity. Anti-tumour mechanism involves upregulation of TNF-α, IL-12, and direct tumour cytotoxicity. SX-fraction increases insulin receptor sensitivity via PPAR-γ activation, reducing blood glucose and insulin resistance.

pharmacokinetics.absorption: Beta-glucan fractions have limited oral bioavailability; partially absorbed in low-molecular-weight fractions and active at gut mucosal level. SX-fraction absorption profile not fully characterised.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: D-fraction distributes to GALT, spleen, and lymph nodes in animal models; higher affinity for lymphoid tissue and tumour microenvironment. SX-fraction distribution to peripheral tissues (muscle, liver, adipose) demonstrated in rodent models. Human tissue distribution data are not available.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: Intestinal microbial fermentation of polysaccharides produces short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs). Absorbed fractions undergo hepatic metabolism.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: Primarily fecal; smaller metabolites via renal route.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: Antidiabetic agents (insulin, metformin, sulphonylureas) — additive hypoglycaemic effect documented. Anticoagulants — theoretical interaction. Immunosuppressants — pharmacodynamic antagonism.
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: LD50 >3 g/kg (oral, rodents). No organ toxicity in chronic animal studies. Human trials up to 6 g/day showed no serious adverse events.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Insufficient clinical data. Avoid concentrated extracts during pregnancy.
special_precautions.lactation: No data on breast milk excretion. Dietary amounts likely safe; supplemental doses — insufficient data.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: No hepatotoxicity documented. Standard monitoring recommended.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: No specific risk identified. No dose adjustment data available.

narrative_summary.historical_use: Native to the Piedade region of Brazil, where it is known as 'Cogumelo do Sol' (mushroom of the sun) or 'Cogumelo de Deus' (mushroom of God). Introduced to Japan in the 1960s, where it became popular after researchers noted low rates of cancer and infections in the Brazilian community that consumed it regularly.
narrative_summary.modern_application: Studied primarily for immunomodulation and anti-tumour activity. Japanese RCTs in gynaecological cancer patients show improved NK cell activity when used as adjunct to chemotherapy. A Norwegian RCT (AndoSan) demonstrated reduced inflammatory markers (TNF-α, IL-6) in healthy subjects. Anti-hyperglycaemic effects documented in type 2 diabetic patients in a small RCT (reduced insulin resistance).
narrative_summary.side_effects: GI upset (nausea, diarrhoea). Rare hepatotoxicity reported (case reports, likely idiosyncratic). Eosinophilia reported in one RCT. Hypoglycaemia risk.
narrative_summary.contraindications: Anticoagulants — potential additive effect. Antidiabetic drugs — additive hypoglycaemia risk. Pre-existing liver disease — use with caution given case reports of hepatotoxicity.

primary_active_compounds: Beta-1,3/1,6-D-glucans (high content, up to 40% dry weight)
clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: Beta-glucans activate macrophages and NK cells via Dectin-1; upregulate Th1 cytokines (IL-12, IFN-γ, TNF-α). Anti-tumour mechanisms include direct cytotoxicity via induction of apoptosis (caspase-3 pathway) and anti-angiogenic effects. Antidiabetic mechanism involves PPAR-γ activation and improved insulin receptor signalling. Agaritine is a potentially carcinogenic hydrazine compound degraded by cooking.

pharmacokinetics.absorption: Beta-glucan fractions partially absorbed; primarily active at GALT. Detailed human PK data not available.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: Beta-glucan fractions concentrate at gut-associated lymphoid tissue (GALT), spleen, and mesenteric lymph nodes; limited systemic distribution. Blazein (steroid compound) distributes broadly due to lipophilicity; plasma protein binding not established in humans. No quantitative human tissue distribution studies available.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: Hepatic metabolism of absorbed fractions. Agaritine hydrolysed by gut bacteria and enzymes; cooking significantly reduces agaritine content.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: Primarily fecal for polysaccharides; renal for smaller metabolites.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: Antidiabetic agents — additive hypoglycaemia risk (documented in RCT). Anticoagulants — theoretical interaction. Immunosuppressants — pharmacodynamic antagonism.
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: LD50 >5 g/kg (oral, rodents). Rare cases of hepatotoxicity in humans (likely idiosyncratic). Agaritine content: raw mushroom contains up to 500 mg/kg; cooking, drying, or storage reduces this substantially. Long-term safety of agaritine exposure in humans not fully characterised.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Insufficient safety data. Presence of agaritine (potentially genotoxic hydrazine derivative) warrants avoidance during pregnancy.
special_precautions.lactation: Insufficient data. Avoid concentrated supplemental preparations during breastfeeding.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: Case reports of hepatotoxicity exist; avoid in patients with hepatic disease or monitor liver enzymes carefully.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: No specific data available. Standard monitoring recommended.

common_name: Snow Fungus / Silver Ear Mushroom / Hvid Gelésvamp

narrative_summary.historical_use: Used in Traditional Chinese Medicine and as a prized culinary delicacy for over 2,000 years. Known in China as 'Bai Mu Er' (white wood ear). Historically associated with female beauty and longevity — legend holds that Yang Guifei, the Tang Dynasty imperial consort famed for her beauty, used it daily. Used medicinally for lung conditions, dry cough, and as a yin tonic.
narrative_summary.modern_application: The primary bioactive — Tremella polysaccharide (TP) — is studied for neuroprotective effects (NGF-like activity), skin hydration (high water-retention capacity comparable to hyaluronic acid), and immunomodulation. Preclinical studies show NGF secretion stimulation and neuroprotection against amyloid-beta toxicity. Cosmetic industry extensively uses TP for topical hydration. Anti-diabetic and anti-atherosclerotic effects in animal models.
narrative_summary.side_effects: Very well tolerated. Rare allergic reactions. No significant adverse effects documented in available clinical literature.
narrative_summary.contraindications: No absolute contraindications established. Caution with immunosuppressants due to immunostimulant properties.

clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: Tremella polysaccharides stimulate NGF secretion in astrocytes (similar mechanism to Lion's Mane), promoting neuronal differentiation and survival. Anti-inflammatory effects via inhibition of NF-κB and COX-2. Skin-hydrating effects attributed to polysaccharide hygroscopic properties and upregulation of hyaluronic acid synthase (HAS2). Immunomodulation via TLR-4 activation.

pharmacokinetics.absorption: Oral bioavailability of TP is low due to high molecular weight. Some fractions absorbed in the GI tract; bioactive effects also mediated at mucosal level. Human PK data very limited.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: High-molecular-weight polysaccharides remain largely confined to the GI tract and GALT; smaller oligosaccharide fragments may enter systemic circulation via lymphatic uptake. Skin distribution is suggested by clinical hydration data, consistent with promotion of dermal hyaluronic acid synthesis. No formal human PK distribution studies.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: Partially fermented by gut microbiota to SCFAs. Absorbed fractions undergo hepatic metabolism.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: Primarily fecal; renal for absorbed small molecular weight fragments.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: No clinically documented drug interactions. Theoretical interaction with anticoagulants and immunosuppressants.
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: LD50 >10 g/kg (oral, mice). No organ toxicity in preclinical studies. Human clinical data very limited but no safety signals identified.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Insufficient human clinical data. Dietary consumption likely safe; concentrated supplements — avoid due to lack of evidence.
special_precautions.lactation: No data on breast milk excretion. Dietary use likely acceptable; supplements — insufficient data.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: No hepatotoxicity documented. Standard precautions apply.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: No specific risk identified. No dose adjustment data.

narrative_summary.historical_use: One of the most prized medicinal mushrooms in East Asian medicine, known as 'Sang Huang' (yellow mulberry) in China and 'Meshimakobu' in Japan. Used for over 2,000 years primarily as an anti-tumour agent, also for haemostasis, dysentery, and as an immune tonic. Grows on mulberry trees (Morus species) and was historically one of the most expensive medicinal fungi.
narrative_summary.modern_application: Strongly investigated for anti-tumour and immunomodulatory properties. Preclinical studies show consistent anti-proliferative effects across multiple cancer cell lines (breast, prostate, colon, leukaemia). Mechanistically among the better-characterised medicinal mushrooms for tumour-suppressive activity. Investigated as an adjunct in prostate cancer in a small human study. Anti-inflammatory properties studied for IBD and arthritis models.
narrative_summary.side_effects: Limited human clinical data. Mild GI symptoms reported. No significant adverse effects documented at typical research doses.
narrative_summary.contraindications: Anticoagulants — additive antiplatelet effect. Immunosuppressants — pharmacodynamic antagonism. Insufficient data for definitive contraindication profile.

clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: Hispidin and related styrylpyrones inhibit PKC (protein kinase C) and scavenge free radicals. Polysaccharides activate macrophages and NK cells via Dectin-1/TLR-4; upregulate IL-12 and IFN-γ. Anti-tumour activity involves TRAIL-mediated apoptosis and VEGF inhibition (anti-angiogenic). Antidiabetic effect via α-glucosidase inhibition and improved insulin sensitivity through PPAR-γ.

pharmacokinetics.absorption: Limited human PK data. Hispidin has demonstrated absorption in animal models; polysaccharide fractions show limited systemic absorption.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: Hispidin and styrylpyrone compounds are lipophilic and distribute widely to liver, adipose tissue, and inflammatory foci. Polysaccharide fractions preferentially target GALT, spleen, and bone marrow. No formal human tissue distribution data available; preclinical rodent data only.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: Hepatic phase I/II metabolism of phenolic compounds assumed. Polysaccharides fermented by gut microbiota.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: Renal excretion of phenolic metabolites; fecal for unabsorbed polysaccharides.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: Anticoagulants — potential additive antiplatelet activity. Immunosuppressants — pharmacodynamic antagonism. No CYP450 drug interactions established in human studies.
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: LD50 >5 g/kg (oral, mice) for polysaccharide extract. No significant organ toxicity in 90-day subacute animal studies. Human safety data very limited.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Insufficient human clinical data. Avoid during pregnancy.
special_precautions.lactation: No data on breast milk excretion. Avoid during breastfeeding.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: No hepatotoxicity documented. Limited data; standard monitoring recommended.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: No specific risk identified. No dose adjustment data available.

narrative_summary.historical_use: One of the most widely used fungi in Traditional Chinese Medicine, mentioned as a primary herb in the Shen Nong Ben Cao Jing (c. 100 CE). Known as 'Fu Ling', used to calm the mind (shen), strengthen the spleen and stomach, promote urination, and resolve dampness. A core component of many classical TCM formulas (e.g., Liu Wei Di Huang Wan, Si Jun Zi Tang).
narrative_summary.modern_application: Beta-pachymaran and pachymic acids are the primary studied bioactives. Documented immunomodulatory, anti-tumour, and anti-inflammatory effects in preclinical studies. Sedative and anxiolytic properties supported by animal pharmacology (interaction with GABA-A receptor). Antidiabetic effects in animal models. Widely used in TCM formulations for GI disorders and insomnia.
narrative_summary.side_effects: Generally very well tolerated. Rare GI symptoms at high doses. Rare hypersensitivity reactions.
narrative_summary.contraindications: Diuretics — additive diuretic effect (Poria has mild diuretic properties). Sedatives/anxiolytics — potential additive CNS depression. No absolute contraindications well-established.

clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: Pachymaran activates macrophages and NK cells via Dectin-1/TLR-4. Pachymic acid inhibits NF-κB pathway, reducing pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α, COX-2). Diuretic action attributed to direct renal tubular effects. Sedative effects involve positive modulation of GABA-A receptor (animal data only).

pharmacokinetics.absorption: Pachymic acid absorbed orally; reaches systemic circulation. Pachymaran (high MW) has limited systemic absorption. Detailed human PK data limited.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: Pachymaran (polysaccharide) preferentially targets GALT and spleen; limited systemic absorption. Pachymic acid (triterpenoid) is lipophilic; distributes to liver and adipose tissue. Plasma protein binding of triterpenoids estimated >80% by analogy with structurally related compounds. No human clinical PK distribution studies.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: Pachymic acid undergoes hepatic CYP450-mediated metabolism. Polysaccharides partially fermented by intestinal microbiota.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: Renal excretion of triterpenoid metabolites; fecal for polysaccharides.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: Diuretics — additive diuretic effects. CNS depressants (benzodiazepines, barbiturates) — potential pharmacodynamic potentiation. Antidiabetic agents — theoretical additive hypoglycaemia.
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: LD50 >10 g/kg (oral, mice) for whole extract. Long-term use considered safe at standard TCM doses. No significant organ toxicity identified in preclinical studies.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Traditionally used cautiously during pregnancy. Insufficient controlled safety data — avoid concentrated preparations.
special_precautions.lactation: No data on breast milk excretion. Avoid supplemental doses during breastfeeding.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: No hepatotoxicity documented. Standard monitoring with concentrated preparations.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: Mild diuretic properties; use with caution in patients on diuretic therapy. No specific dose adjustment data.

narrative_summary.historical_use: Known in TCM as 'Zhu Ling', used for over 2,000 years primarily as a diuretic and for treating oedema, urinary difficulty, and diarrhoea. Referenced in classical texts including Shen Nong Ben Cao Jing. The sclerotium grows on the roots of deciduous trees. A major component of the classical TCM formula 'Zhu Ling Tang' for urinary tract disorders.
narrative_summary.modern_application: Ergone (a steroid from the fruiting body) and polysaccharides (PU-PS) are the primary bioactives. Zhu Ling polysaccharide injection (ZPS) is used in China as an adjunct to chemotherapy in bladder and other cancers. Diuretic effect well-documented. Immunomodulatory properties studied in animal models and small clinical trials. Anti-tumour properties via NK cell and macrophage activation.
narrative_summary.side_effects: Generally well tolerated. Electrolyte imbalance possible with prolonged high-dose use due to diuretic effect. Rare GI symptoms.
narrative_summary.contraindications: Diuretics — additive electrolyte-depleting effect. Antihypertensives — additive hypotensive effect. Lithium — diuretic-mediated lithium retention.

clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: PU-PS activates macrophages and T-cells via TLR-2/Dectin-1; stimulates IL-2, IFN-γ. Anti-tumour effects via NK cell activation and induction of tumour cell apoptosis (Bcl-2 suppression). Diuretic mechanism: inhibition of renal Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase and modulation of aquaporin expression. Ergosterol is a precursor to vitamin D2.

pharmacokinetics.absorption: Limited human PK data. Polysaccharides have limited systemic oral bioavailability. Ergone absorption profile not characterised in humans.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: Polysaccharide fractions (PU-PS) concentrate in kidneys, spleen, and lymph nodes, consistent with diuretic and immunomodulatory effects. Ergosterol derivatives distribute systemically. No formal human pharmacokinetic distribution studies available.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: Assumed hepatic metabolism of steroid (ergone) compounds. Polysaccharides fermented in colon.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: Renal excretion (diuretic mechanism increases urinary output). Fecal for unabsorbed polysaccharides.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: Diuretics (thiazides, loop diuretics) — additive effect and electrolyte imbalance risk. Antihypertensives — additive hypotension. Lithium — may increase lithium serum levels by reducing renal clearance.
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: LD50 >5 g/kg (oral, rodents). No significant organ toxicity in animal studies. Human clinical data limited; ZPS injection well-tolerated in cancer adjunct trials.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Insufficient clinical data. Diuretic properties could theoretically affect fluid balance. Avoid during pregnancy.
special_precautions.lactation: No data available. Avoid during breastfeeding.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: No hepatotoxicity documented. Standard monitoring recommended.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: Diuretic properties require caution in renal impairment; monitor electrolytes and renal function.

narrative_summary.historical_use: Widely consumed as food in East Asia for centuries. Known in Japan as 'Enokitake', in China as 'Jin Zhen Gu' (golden needle mushroom). Used in TCM for liver disease, gastric disorders, and as a tonic. The wild form (brown, thick-stemmed) differs significantly in bioactive content from the commercially cultivated white, slender variety.
narrative_summary.modern_application: Profungin / FVE (Flammulina velutipes immunomodulatory protein) is a unique ribosome-inactivating lectin with anti-tumour and immunomodulatory properties, studied in animal models. Flammutoxin, a cardiotoxic protein in the raw mushroom, is destroyed by cooking. Flammulin (a lectin) demonstrates anti-tumour activity. Ergothioneine content is among the highest of edible mushrooms. Epidemiological data from Japan links high enoki consumption to reduced cancer incidence.
narrative_summary.side_effects: Raw mushroom consumption can cause GI irritation and potential cardiotoxicity (flammutoxin) — must be cooked. Cooked or dried preparations are safe. Rare hypersensitivity reactions.
narrative_summary.contraindications: Raw consumption contraindicated. Rare mushroom allergy. No other well-established contraindications at dietary levels.

clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: FVE activates macrophages and NK cells; stimulates Th1 cytokines (IFN-γ, IL-2). Flammulin inhibits protein synthesis in cancer cells via ribosome inactivating protein (RIP)-like activity. Lectins act as TLR-4 ligands, activating innate immunity. Proflamin targets CD4+ T-cells as an immunomodulatory lectin.

pharmacokinetics.absorption: Ergothioneine absorbed via organic cation transporter OCTN1; accumulates selectively in tissues under oxidative stress. FVE and flammulin are proteins largely degraded during digestion; immunological activity may be mediated at mucosal level.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: FVE (Flammulina velutipes lectin) and high-MW polysaccharides primarily act at mucosal surfaces and GALT. Flammulin distributes preferentially to actively dividing cells. Ergothioneine accumulates in erythrocytes via the OCTN1 transporter. No formal human PK distribution data.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: Ergothioneine: not significantly metabolised; retained in erythrocytes and mitochondria-rich tissues. Protein bioactives: digested to peptides/amino acids.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: Ergothioneine: renal excretion of intact molecule and metabolites.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: No established clinically significant drug interactions. Theoretical interaction with anticoagulants (antiplatelet activity reported in vitro).
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: Flammutoxin is heat-labile and destroyed at cooking temperatures (>70°C); cooked preparations are non-toxic. LD50 of whole cooked extract >5 g/kg in rodents. No organ toxicity documented with cooked/extracted forms.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Dietary consumption of cooked mushroom considered safe. Raw consumption must be avoided. Concentrated extracts — insufficient data.
special_precautions.lactation: Dietary cooked consumption likely safe. No data on concentrated extract transfer to breast milk.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: No hepatotoxicity documented with cooked preparations. Standard monitoring with concentrated extracts.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: No specific risk identified. Ergothioneine is renally excreted; accumulation possible in severe renal failure — monitor.

narrative_summary.historical_use: Consumed as food across Europe and Asia for centuries. Less prominent in traditional medicine than Asian medicinal fungi, but used in Eastern European folk medicine for general health. One of the most widely cultivated mushrooms globally, appreciated for culinary and nutritional value. References to medicinal use in Slavic folk medicine for liver and cardiovascular conditions.
narrative_summary.modern_application: Among edible fungi, oyster mushrooms have the most clinical evidence for lipid-lowering effects. A placebo-controlled RCT demonstrated significant reduction in total cholesterol, LDL, and triglycerides in hyperlipidaemic patients. Lovastatin (mevinolin) content — a genuine statin — is the likely cholesterol-lowering mechanism. Antidiabetic effects (alpha-glucosidase inhibition) and immunomodulatory properties documented. Pleuran (beta-1,3/1,6-glucan) from P. ostreatus is commercially used as an immunomodulatory supplement.
narrative_summary.side_effects: Generally very well tolerated. Rare contact dermatitis (occupational). Rare asthma with spore inhalation. Possible GI intolerance with very large consumption.
narrative_summary.contraindications: Statins (atorvastatin, simvastatin) — additive lipid-lowering effect and potential for myopathy due to lovastatin content. Caution in patients with myopathy. Immunosuppressants — pharmacodynamic antagonism (pleuran).

primary_active_compounds: Lovastatin (mevinolin) — 2.8% dry weight in some varieties
clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: Lovastatin competitively inhibits HMG-CoA reductase, reducing hepatic cholesterol synthesis and upregulating LDL receptor expression. Beta-glucans activate Dectin-1, promoting Th1 immune response. ACE-inhibitory peptides reduce angiotensin II formation, producing antihypertensive effects. Ergothioneine acts as antioxidant via Nrf2/HO-1 upregulation.

pharmacokinetics.absorption: Lovastatin absorbed in prodrug (lactone) form; hydrolysed to active hydroxy acid in gut/liver; bioavailability ~5% due to first-pass effect. Pleuran: limited systemic absorption; active at mucosal/GALT level.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: Lovastatin undergoes extensive hepatic first-pass metabolism; the active β-hydroxy acid metabolite distributes systemically with high hepatic uptake (target organ) and ~95% plasma protein binding. Ergothioneine concentrates in erythrocytes via OCTN1 transporter. Polysaccharide fractions confined to GI/GALT. Volume of distribution of lovastatin ~0.5 L/kg.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: Lovastatin extensively metabolised by CYP3A4 to active and inactive metabolites. Pleuran fermented by gut microbiota.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: Lovastatin: primarily biliary/fecal (83%); minor renal (10%). Pleuran: fecal.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: Statins (atorvastatin, simvastatin) — additive HMG-CoA inhibition; combined use may increase risk of myopathy/rhabdomyolysis. CYP3A4 inhibitors (azole antifungals, macrolide antibiotics) — increase lovastatin exposure and myopathy risk. Anticoagulants (warfarin) — lovastatin inhibits warfarin metabolism; monitor INR.
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: Whole mushroom: LD50 not established; dietary consumption is safe. Concentrated lovastatin-rich extracts carry statin-class adverse effects (myopathy, elevated liver enzymes) at pharmacological doses.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Dietary consumption safe. Lovastatin is contraindicated during pregnancy (Category X in standard statin classification — teratogenic in animal models). Avoid high-dose concentrated extracts.
special_precautions.lactation: Lovastatin excreted in breast milk. Concentrated lovastatin-containing preparations should be avoided during breastfeeding.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: Lovastatin is contraindicated in active liver disease. Monitoring of liver enzymes with regular high-dose consumption is prudent.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: Lovastatin primarily excreted biliary; mild renal impairment generally does not require dose adjustment. Severe renal impairment — use with caution.

narrative_summary.historical_use: Consumed as food in parts of Asia, Africa, and Latin America. Used in traditional medicine in Mexico and Southeast Asia for fever, headache, and infections. One of the most widespread mushroom species globally (found on every continent except Antarctica). Not traditionally prominent in Chinese or Japanese medicinal systems, but significant in modern Japanese oncology research.
narrative_summary.modern_application: Schizophyllan (SPG / Sonifilan / sizofiran) — a beta-1,3-glucan — is approved in Japan as an adjunct to radiation therapy in cervical cancer, significantly improving survival. Clinical trials demonstrate immune stimulation with improved NK cell and lymphocyte activity. Intratumoral administration also investigated. Antibacterial and antifungal properties noted in vitro. Interestingly, it is an opportunistic pathogen in immunocompromised hosts (sino-pulmonary infections).
narrative_summary.side_effects: IV/intratumoral administration: local injection reactions, mild fever. Oral use: generally well tolerated. Note: can cause opportunistic infections (schizophyllosis) in severely immunocompromised patients.
narrative_summary.contraindications: Severe immunocompromise (risk of opportunistic infection). Immunosuppressants — complex pharmacodynamic interaction.

clinical_data.used_part: Fruiting body; SPG (schizophyllan) derived from fermentation of mycelium
clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: Schizophyllan activates macrophages and NK cells via Dectin-1 receptor; upregulates IL-2, IFN-γ, TNF-α. Augments T-lymphocyte and dendritic cell function. Anti-tumour mechanism involves host-mediated immunostimulation rather than direct cytotoxicity. TLR-2/Dectin-1 co-activation amplifies innate immune signalling cascades.

pharmacokinetics.absorption: Oral bioavailability of high-MW schizophyllan is very limited; clinical use is parenteral (intramuscular or intratumoral). Oral formulations active at mucosal immune level.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: Schizophyllan (SPG) is a high-molecular-weight β-glucan; following clinical (IV/IM) administration in Japan, it distributes to spleen, lymph nodes, and tumour-associated lymphatics. Oral bioavailability is poor; clinically relevant distribution data applies to parenteral routes only. No meaningful systemic oral distribution.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: Partially hydrolysed by gut glucanases; absorbed fractions undergo hepatic metabolism.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: Primarily fecal for oral forms; renal for parenteral metabolites.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: Immunosuppressants — pharmacodynamic antagonism. Radiation therapy — used as adjunct (synergistic, not pharmacokinetic). No established CYP450 interactions.
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: SPG parenteral: well-tolerated in clinical trials; injection site reactions. Risk of opportunistic infection (Schizophyllum commune sino-pulmonary infection) in immunocompromised individuals — this is a documented pathogen, not just a theoretical risk.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Insufficient human clinical data. Avoid during pregnancy.
special_precautions.lactation: No data available. Avoid during breastfeeding.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: No hepatotoxicity documented. Standard monitoring recommended.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: No specific risk identified. No dose adjustment data.

narrative_summary.historical_use: Used in Traditional Chinese Medicine for over 1,000 years, known as 'Mu Er' (wood ear) or 'Hei Mu Er' (black wood ear). Traditionally used for improving blood circulation, treating throat conditions, and as a yin tonic for the lungs and stomach. A common food ingredient in Asian cuisine (soups, stir-fries). Reference to its use for blood disorders found in Ben Cao Gang Mu (Li Shizhen, 1596).
narrative_summary.modern_application: Clinically relevant for its significant anticoagulant and antiplatelet effects — documented in multiple controlled studies. A 1980 observation by Andrew Weil noted increased bleeding in patients consuming large quantities of Chinese food (black fungus); subsequent research confirmed potent platelet inhibition. Antidiabetic effects via alpha-glucosidase inhibition documented. Cholesterol-lowering and anti-atherosclerotic properties in animal models. Immunomodulatory polysaccharides (AAP) studied preclinically.
narrative_summary.side_effects: Significant anticoagulant/antiplatelet effect — risk of bleeding, especially with concurrent anticoagulant use. GI upset with large quantities. Rare hypersensitivity.
narrative_summary.contraindications: Concurrent use with anticoagulants or antiplatelets (warfarin, aspirin, clopidogrel, heparin) — clinically significant bleeding risk. Pre-surgical period — discontinue at least 2 weeks before surgery. Haemophilia or bleeding disorders.

primary_active_compounds: Auricularia auricula polysaccharides (AAP — beta-glucans, acidic heteropolysaccharides)
clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: Adenosine and AAP inhibit platelet aggregation via adenosine A2A receptor activation, increasing platelet cAMP and reducing thromboxane A2. Anticoagulant polysaccharides inhibit thrombin and factor Xa (heparin-like mechanism). Hypoglycaemic activity via α-glucosidase inhibition and GLUT4 upregulation in skeletal muscle. Immunomodulation via TLR-4/Dectin-1.

pharmacokinetics.absorption: Adenosine rapidly absorbed and metabolised. Polysaccharide fractions limited systemic absorption; active at mucosal level. Human PK data for whole extract not available.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: Adenosine distributes rapidly to red blood cells and vascular endothelium via active transport; plasma half-life ~10 seconds. High-MW anticoagulant polysaccharides distribute to vascular endothelium. Ergosterol distributes to lipid-rich membranes. No formal human PK studies for the fungal extract as a whole.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: Adenosine rapidly deaminated by adenosine deaminase to inosine. Polysaccharides fermented by gut microbiota.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: Renal excretion of adenosine metabolites; fecal for polysaccharides.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: Anticoagulants (warfarin, heparin) and antiplatelets (aspirin, clopidogrel, NSAIDs) — CLINICALLY SIGNIFICANT additive bleeding risk, documented in case reports. NSAIDs — potential additive GI bleeding risk.
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: Generally safe at dietary amounts. At pharmacological concentrations the antiplatelet effect is the primary safety concern. No significant organ toxicity in animal studies.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Anticoagulant properties raise concern for increased bleeding risk during pregnancy, particularly peripartum. Avoid concentrated preparations; dietary quantities with medical supervision.
special_precautions.lactation: No data on breast milk excretion. Dietary use likely safe; concentrated preparations — insufficient data.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: No hepatotoxicity documented. If hepatic coagulopathy is present, additive bleeding risk from antiplatelet effect — use with caution.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: No specific risk identified. Adenosine metabolism may be affected in severe renal impairment; monitor.

common_name: Paryk-blækhat / Shaggy Mane / Shaggy Ink Cap

narrative_summary.historical_use: Eaten as a culinary mushroom in Europe and North America, known as the shaggy ink cap. Not historically prominent in TCM but used in Chinese folk medicine under the name 'Ji Tui Mo'. Recognised for its edibility (must be consumed promptly after harvest as it auto-digests rapidly). Traditional use in Chinese folk medicine for improved digestion and energy.
narrative_summary.modern_application: Studied primarily for antidiabetic properties. Polysaccharides and small molecules from C. comatus have demonstrated significant hypoglycaemic effects in diabetic animal models, comparable to metformin in some studies. Immunomodulatory and anti-tumour properties documented in vitro and in vivo. Vanadium content (a known insulin-mimetic mineral) may contribute to antidiabetic effect. Coprinin and related compounds under early investigation.
narrative_summary.side_effects: Generally safe when consumed cooked. Coprine (found in related Coprinus species — notably C. atramentarius, NOT C. comatus) causes disulfiram-like reaction with alcohol; this compound is absent in C. comatus. Rare GI symptoms. Do not confuse species.
narrative_summary.contraindications: Species confusion risk (C. atramentarius contains coprine — disulfiram-like reaction with alcohol). C. comatus itself does not contain coprine. Antidiabetic drugs — potential additive hypoglycaemia. No other established contraindications.

clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: Polysaccharides stimulate insulin secretion from pancreatic beta-cells and improve insulin sensitivity via PPAR-γ activation; inhibit α-glucosidase and aldose reductase. Lectins act as TLR-4 agonists, promoting macrophage activation. Ink-cap polysaccharides upregulate Nrf2/HO-1, providing antioxidant protection in hyperglycaemic conditions. Coprinol exhibits antimicrobial activity against MRSA.

pharmacokinetics.absorption: Vanadium absorption: 0.5–2% of oral dose; accumulates in bone, liver, kidney. Polysaccharide fractions: limited systemic absorption, active at mucosal level. Human PK data for whole extract unavailable.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: Polysaccharide fractions concentrate at GALT, pancreatic tissue, and spleen, consistent with antidiabetic and immunomodulatory activity. Vanadium-containing compounds (if present) distribute to bone and kidney. No formal human pharmacokinetic distribution data available for this species.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: Vanadium: reduction from V(V) to V(IV) in gut; hepatic metabolism. Polysaccharides: gut microbial fermentation.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: Vanadium: primarily renal; polysaccharides: fecal.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: Antidiabetic agents (insulin, metformin, sulphonylureas) — additive hypoglycaemic risk. Anticoagulants — theoretical platelet inhibition. Iron supplements — vanadium may interfere with iron absorption.
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: LD50 of whole extract >5 g/kg (oral, rodents). Vanadium toxicity: high doses (not achievable from dietary mushroom) cause GI irritation, renal accumulation; irrelevant at culinary quantities. No clinically significant organ toxicity documented.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Insufficient data on concentrated extracts. Dietary consumption likely safe. Vanadium at pharmacological doses is not recommended in pregnancy.
special_precautions.lactation: No data on breast milk excretion. Dietary consumption likely safe; concentrated extracts — insufficient data.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: No hepatotoxicity documented. Standard monitoring recommended.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: Vanadium excreted renally; accumulation possible in renal impairment. Avoid high-dose concentrated preparations in renal failure.

narrative_summary.historical_use: Endemic to Taiwan, where it grows exclusively on the inner cavity of Cinnamomum kanehirai (Taiwanese sassafras). Known locally as 'Zhang Zhi' or 'Niu Zhang Zhi'. Used in Taiwanese indigenous medicine and folk medicine for centuries as a treatment for liver disease, abdominal pain, diarrhoea, and as a hangover remedy. Historically one of the most expensive medicinal fungi in Taiwan.
narrative_summary.modern_application: Hepatoprotective and anti-tumour properties are the primary research focus. Clinical evidence supports use as a hepatoprotective agent in patients with chronic hepatitis and liver fibrosis (several Taiwanese RCTs). Anti-proliferative effects documented across multiple cancer cell lines. Antroquinonol (a unique ubiquinone derivative) has entered Phase II clinical trials in Taiwan for lung and pancreatic cancer. Strong anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory activities.
narrative_summary.side_effects: Generally well tolerated. Mild GI symptoms. Rare hypersensitivity. Potential QT interval prolongation with high doses (preclinical).
narrative_summary.contraindications: Hepatotoxic drugs — monitor liver function. Anticoagulants — theoretical additive effect. Drugs affecting QT interval — use with caution (preclinical signal).

clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: Antroquinonol inhibits Ras/Akt/mTOR signalling, inducing tumour cell autophagy and apoptosis; also inhibits HIF-1α, reducing hypoxia-driven VEGF expression (anti-angiogenic). Triterpenoids inhibit NF-κB pathway, reducing IL-6, TNF-α, and COX-2. Hepatoprotective via Nrf2/HO-1 upregulation and suppression of CYP2E1-mediated oxidative stress.

pharmacokinetics.absorption: Antroquinonol bioavailability ~20% in rodents. Triterpenoid absorption limited; improved by lipid-based formulation. Human PK data emerging from Phase I/II trials.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: Antroquinonol and ubiquinone derivatives are highly lipophilic; distribute to tumour vasculature, liver, and adipose tissue. Significant hepatic first-pass metabolism expected. Half-life of antroquinonol estimated at ~4–6 hours in preclinical models. No formal human PK distribution data available.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: Hepatic CYP3A4-mediated metabolism of triterpenoids. Antroquinonol undergoes phase II conjugation.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: Biliary/fecal for triterpenoids; renal for water-soluble metabolites.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: CYP3A4 substrates — potential inhibition by triterpenoids. Anticoagulants — theoretical additive anticoagulant effect. Hepatotoxic drugs — monitor; paradoxically, may be hepatoprotective in certain contexts.
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: LD50 >5 g/kg for whole extract (rodents). Antroquinonol Phase I trial showed acceptable safety profile. QT prolongation signal at suprapharmacological doses in preclinical studies — clinical significance unclear.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Insufficient clinical data. Potent bioactivity warrants avoidance during pregnancy.
special_precautions.lactation: No data on breast milk excretion. Avoid during breastfeeding.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: Paradox: hepatoprotective in documented trials, but CYP metabolism may be impaired; monitor carefully and start at low doses.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: No specific data. Standard monitoring recommended.

narrative_summary.historical_use: One of the oldest known medicinal fungi, used by humans for at least 5,300 years — a piece was found in the toolkit of Ötzi the Iceman (Alpine mummy, c. 3,300 BCE), likely used as a fire-starting material and possibly for medicinal purposes. Used in European folk medicine for wound healing, as a haemostatic agent, and to treat digestive disorders. The amadou (processed inner tissue) was used historically as tinder, clothing, and wound dressings.
narrative_summary.modern_application: Contemporary research has confirmed antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and cytotoxic properties. Fomentariol and related triterpenoids show anti-tumour activity in vitro. Polysaccharides (fomentosan) demonstrate immunomodulatory effects. Beta-glucans contribute to immunostimulatory activity. Primarily studied in vitro and in animal models; human clinical data are absent. Growing interest as a scaffold for biomaterials and wound-care applications.
narrative_summary.side_effects: Insufficient human clinical data to characterise adverse effects fully. No significant toxicity reported in preclinical studies. Theoretical bleeding risk with concentrated extracts.
narrative_summary.contraindications: No well-established clinical contraindications due to lack of human data. Anticoagulants — theoretical additive anticoagulant activity. Avoid in pregnancy due to lack of safety data.

clinical_data.used_part: Fruiting body (conk), particularly the inner trama tissue (amadou)
clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: Hispidin and fomentariol act as potent antioxidants (radical scavenging via phenolic hydroxyl groups). Fomentariol inhibits topoisomerase II, inducing cancer cell cycle arrest (G2/M phase). Polysaccharide fractions activate Dectin-1/TLR-4, promoting IL-12 and TNF-α secretion. Anticoagulant activity through inhibition of thrombin and platelet aggregation.

pharmacokinetics.absorption: No human PK data available. Phenolic compounds expected to have moderate oral absorption; triterpenoids limited due to poor water solubility.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: Fomentariol and hispidin are phenolic compounds with moderate lipophilicity; distribute systemically after intestinal absorption. Polysaccharide fractions concentrate in GALT and spleen. No formal human pharmacokinetic distribution data; all available data from in vitro and rodent studies.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: Assumed hepatic phase I/II metabolism of phenolic and triterpenoid compounds based on analogous structures.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: Assumed primarily biliary/fecal for triterpenoids; renal for phenolic metabolites.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: No clinically documented interactions. Theoretical interaction with anticoagulants based on in vitro antiplatelet activity.
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: LD50 not established in humans. No significant organ toxicity in preclinical studies at normal doses. Amadou (physical material) used as wound dressing is non-toxic.

special_precautions.pregnancy: No human safety data. Avoid during pregnancy.
special_precautions.lactation: No human safety data. Avoid during breastfeeding.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: No hepatotoxicity documented. No data in hepatic impairment.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: No data available. Standard caution applies.

common_name: Ophiocordyceps sinensis / Wild Tibetan Caterpillar Fungus

narrative_summary.historical_use: The taxonomically correct name for the wild-harvested Tibetan caterpillar fungus, previously classified as Cordyceps sinensis. Formed from parasitism of Hepialus moth larvae at altitudes >3,500 m on the Tibetan Plateau. Documented in Tibetan medical texts since the 15th century as a potent tonic for fatigue, sexual vitality, respiratory capacity, and kidney function. Among the most expensive natural substances per gram globally.
narrative_summary.modern_application: Clinically validated in Chinese RCTs for chronic kidney disease (reduced proteinuria, creatinine stabilisation). Immune modulation in transplant patients. Anti-fatigue and VO2-max improvement documented in elderly subjects. Because wild harvesting is unsustainable and prohibitively expensive, nearly all modern research now uses the Cs-4 cultured mycelium (still often labelled 'Cordyceps sinensis'). Aqueous and ethanolic extracts studied for anti-tumour properties.
narrative_summary.side_effects: Mild GI upset. Rare hypersensitivity. Potential hypoglycaemia with antidiabetic co-administration. Bleeding risk with anticoagulants.
narrative_summary.contraindications: Anticoagulants. Immunosuppressants (cyclosporine). Autoimmune conditions — caution. Pre-surgery.

clinical_data.used_part: Whole fruiting body-larvae complex (wild); Cs-4 mycelium (cultured, commercially used)
clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: Cordycepin inhibits mRNA polyadenylation, blocking cancer cell proliferation. Adenosine receptor modulation regulates cardiac output and vascular tone. HIF-1α stabilisation improves tissue oxygenation under hypoxic conditions. Cordycepin activates AMPK, enhancing glucose uptake in muscle. Th1/Th2 balance regulation via IL-12/IL-10 axis modulation.

pharmacokinetics.absorption: Same profile as cultured Cordyceps — cordycepin rapidly deaminated. Unique wild-type peptides: oral bioavailability likely very low due to GI degradation.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: Cordycepin distributes rapidly to lung, kidney, liver, and immune tissues. CSE-PS (polysaccharide) concentrates in spleen and thymus. Adenosine has a very short plasma half-life (~10 seconds) due to active cellular uptake. Plasma protein binding of nucleoside compounds is generally low (<30%). Pharmacokinetics closely parallel those of Cordyceps sinensis (Cs-4).
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: Hepatic metabolism; adenosine deaminase-mediated for cordycepin.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: Renal excretion of purine metabolites.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: Cyclosporine (the immunosuppressant) — potential pharmacodynamic interaction as the fungus contains cyclosporin analogues. Anticoagulants, antidiabetics — same as Cordyceps sinensis.
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: Wild form: same general profile as Cs-4. Heavy metal contamination has been documented in some wild specimens from polluted regions — an important quality concern. Cs-4 cultured mycelium has established safety data; wild form less well studied in controlled settings.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Insufficient data. Avoid during pregnancy.
special_precautions.lactation: Insufficient data. Avoid during breastfeeding.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: No hepatotoxicity documented. Standard monitoring recommended.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: Studied as renoprotective agent in CKD. Monitor renal function during use. Caution with severe impairment.

narrative_summary.historical_use: Known in Traditional Chinese Medicine as 'Wu Ling Shen' (the fungus found inside the chambers of ancient termite mounds). Used historically for insomnia, anxiety, and to calm the mind. Referenced in TCM pharmacopeias as a sedative tonic. Grows inside the nests of Odontotermes formosanus (Formosan subterranean termite) in a unique symbiotic relationship.
narrative_summary.modern_application: Studied primarily for sedative and anxiolytic properties. Animal studies demonstrate significant reduction in sleep latency and increase in sleep duration, comparable to diazepam in some models. GABA-A receptor modulation is the proposed primary mechanism. Neuroprotective effects against oxidative stress documented in vitro. Commercial products marketed in Taiwan and China for insomnia and anxiety. Human clinical data remain very limited.
narrative_summary.side_effects: Insufficient human clinical data. Potential sedation and CNS depression. No significant toxicity reported in preclinical studies.
narrative_summary.contraindications: CNS depressants (benzodiazepines, alcohol, barbiturates, opioids) — potential additive sedation. Caution when operating machinery. Insufficient data for comprehensive contraindication profile.

primary_active_compounds: N/A — specific active compounds not yet fully characterised
clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: Positive allosteric modulation of GABA-A receptor (benzodiazepine-like effect), prolonging inhibitory chloride channel opening. 5-HT reuptake inhibition proposed in animal models. Anti-inflammatory effects via NF-κB inhibition and reduction of IL-6, TNF-α. Adenosine receptor modulation may contribute to anxiolytic and sleep-promoting effects.

pharmacokinetics.absorption: No human PK data available. Bioavailability of active constituents not characterised.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: Active alkaloids and sesquiterpenes are lipophilic; cross the blood-brain barrier in animal studies and distribute broadly to CNS, with preference for limbic structures (amygdala, hippocampus). No formal human PK distribution data; all data from rodent sleep and anxiety models.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: Not established.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: Not established.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: CNS depressants — additive sedative effect (theoretical, based on GABA-A mechanism). Alcohol — potential potentiation of CNS depression. No drug interaction studies in humans.
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: LD50 not established for human use. Preclinical animal studies show no significant organ toxicity at doses up to 5 g/kg. Human safety data absent.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Insufficient data. Avoid during pregnancy.
special_precautions.lactation: Insufficient data. Avoid during breastfeeding.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: No data available.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: No data available.

narrative_summary.historical_use: A fungal pathogen of maize (Zea mays), consumed as a food delicacy in Mesoamerica for thousands of years, known as 'huitlacoche' or 'cuitlacoche' in Mexico. Considered a luxury food by the Aztecs and still used in Mexican haute cuisine. Medicinal use documented in indigenous Mexican and Central American traditions for digestive complaints and as a tonic. The infected corn galls are consumed as a vegetable.
narrative_summary.modern_application: Research focus is primarily on its exceptional nutritional profile (high in lysine, an amino acid limiting in maize; high in unsaturated fatty acids and fibre) and its bioactive compounds. Ustilagic acid (a sophorolipid-like glycolipid) shows anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties in vitro. Indole compounds (including indole-3-acetic acid) may have anti-tumour activity. Under increasing study as a functional food and fermentation substrate. Human clinical data for medicinal applications are absent.
narrative_summary.side_effects: Generally considered safe as a food. Allergic reactions possible in persons with corn or mould allergies. No significant toxicity documented.
narrative_summary.contraindications: Corn or mould allergy. No established pharmacological contraindications.

clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: Ustilagic acid disrupts bacterial and fungal cell membranes (detergent-like mechanism) and inhibits NF-κB-mediated pro-inflammatory signalling. Ergothioneine accumulates via OCTN1 transporter; protects mitochondria and DNA from oxidative damage. Polysaccharides activate Dectin-1/TLR-4, promoting macrophage differentiation. Rich in melanin precursors with UV-protective properties.

pharmacokinetics.absorption: No human PK data for isolated bioactives. Food matrix absorption of phenolics is moderate. Indole compounds absorbed via passive diffusion.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: Ustilagic acid and lipid compounds are highly lipophilic; distribute to lipid-rich membranes and adipose tissue. Ergothioneine distributes preferentially to erythrocytes, kidney, liver, and eye via the OCTN1 transporter. Polysaccharides confined to GI/GALT. No formal PK distribution data.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: Hepatic conjugation of phenolic compounds; indole metabolism via CYP1A2.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: Renal excretion of conjugated phenolics.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: No documented drug interactions. CYP1A2 substrates — theoretical interaction via indole metabolism.
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: Consumed safely as food for millennia at culinary quantities. No LD50 data for concentrated extracts. No organ toxicity identified in available studies.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Dietary consumption as food considered safe. Concentrated medicinal preparations — insufficient data.
special_precautions.lactation: Dietary consumption likely safe. No data for concentrated preparations.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: No hepatotoxicity documented. Standard precautions for concentrated preparations.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: No specific risk identified.

common_name: Klinisk psykiatrisk forskning / Magic Mushroom (psilocybin source)

narrative_summary.historical_use: Used in Mesoamerican indigenous ceremonial and religious contexts for thousands of years, particularly by the Mazatec people of Oaxaca, Mexico (known as 'teonanácatl' — 'flesh of the gods'). Psilocybin was first isolated and synthesised by Albert Hofmann at Sandoz in 1958. Studied in the 1960s before being classified as Schedule I in the US in 1970, halting research for decades.
narrative_summary.modern_application: Psilocybin (the prodrug) and psilocin (the active metabolite) are the subject of renewed rigorous clinical research. Phase II/III trials from Johns Hopkins, Imperial College London, and COMPASS Pathways show significant efficacy in treatment-resistant depression (FDA Breakthrough Therapy Designation), major depressive disorder, and end-of-life existential distress in cancer patients. Single-dose or two-dose protocols produce durable antidepressant effects for 6–12 months in responders. Promising data in alcohol and nicotine use disorders.
