narrative_summary.side_effects: Acute (dose-dependent): perceptual distortions, synesthesia, ego dissolution, anxiety, confusion, tachycardia, hypertension, nausea. Challenging psychological experiences ('bad trips') — risk mitigated by psychological support/set-and-setting. Headache post-session. Hallucinogen persisting perception disorder (HPPD) — rare. No physical withdrawal syndrome. Non-addictive.
narrative_summary.contraindications: Personal or family history of schizophrenia, bipolar I disorder, or other psychotic disorders — absolute contraindication (risk of precipitating psychosis). Concurrent lithium — risk of seizures and hyperthermia (documented case reports). MAOIs — risk of serotonin syndrome. Uncontrolled cardiovascular disease — relative contraindication (transient hypertension/tachycardia).

clinical_data.used_part: Fruiting body (as pharmacological source); isolated psilocybin used in clinical trials
clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: Psilocybin is rapidly dephosphorylated to psilocin by alkaline phosphatase. Psilocin is a full agonist at serotonin 5-HT2A receptor in cortical pyramidal neurons, disrupting default mode network (DMN) activity. Also agonist at 5-HT2C and 5-HT1A. Promotes neuroplasticity via BDNF upregulation and synaptogenesis. AMPA/NMDA receptor modulation contributes to sustained antidepressant effect.

pharmacokinetics.absorption: Psilocybin: oral bioavailability ~50%. Tmax ~1.5h. Rapidly converted to psilocin.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: Psilocybin is rapidly converted to psilocin in the gut and liver by alkaline phosphatase. Psilocin (active form) is lipophilic; crosses the blood-brain barrier readily and distributes broadly to cortex, thalamus, and limbic structures (high 5-HT2A receptor density). Plasma protein binding ~64%. Volume of distribution estimated ~0.8 L/kg. Half-life of psilocin ~3 hours.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: Psilocin undergoes glucuronidation (primary) and oxidative metabolism via monoamine oxidase (MAO) and CYP2D6/CYP3A4. Half-life of psilocin ~2–3 hours.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: Primarily renal as psilocin-glucuronide (>50% of dose within 24h). Minimal unchanged psilocin in urine.

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: Lithium carbonate — risk of seizures and hyperthermia (case reports of life-threatening interactions). MAOIs — significantly prolong and intensify effects; risk of serotonin syndrome. SSRIs — blunt acute psychedelic effects (5-HT2A downregulation); not thought to be dangerous but may reduce efficacy. Tramadol — serotonin syndrome risk.
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: Physiological toxicity very low: LD50 estimated at 280 mg/kg (oral, rats) — far above any human dose. No organ toxicity documented. Psychological risk (acute anxiety, rare HPPD) is the dominant safety concern. Psilocybin is physiologically non-addictive and produces rapid tolerance.

special_precautions.pregnancy: No human data. Avoid in pregnancy. Animal studies show no teratogenicity at sub-psychedelic doses, but clinical use in pregnant individuals is contraindicated.
special_precautions.lactation: No human data on breast milk excretion. Contraindicated during breastfeeding.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: Hepatic glucuronidation is the primary metabolic pathway; significant hepatic impairment may increase psilocin exposure. No clinical dose-adjustment data available.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: Primary excretion is renal; renal impairment may prolong psilocin exposure. No clinical dose-adjustment data. Monitor carefully.

narrative_summary.historical_use: One of the most culturally iconic mushrooms globally. Used in shamanic traditions across Siberia and northern Europe for millennia as an entheogen. Historical use by the Koryak, Chukchi, and other Siberian shamans; speculated to be the 'Soma' of the Rigveda (Gordon Wasson's hypothesis, contested). The active compounds ibotenic acid and muscimol are entirely distinct from the psilocybin found in Psilocybe species. Culturally associated with Christmas/Yule traditions in northern Europe.
narrative_summary.modern_application: Interest is strictly pharmacological and research-oriented. Muscimol is a potent, selective GABA-A receptor agonist studied as a research tool. Not approved as a therapeutic. Ibotenic acid is a potent glutamate (NMDA/AMPA) receptor agonist used experimentally to create animal models of neurodegeneration. Muscimol has been investigated (preclinically) for epilepsy, anxiety, and pain. Microdosing with Amanita muscaria extracts is a growing, unregulated consumer trend — without clinical safety data.
narrative_summary.side_effects: TOXIC if consumed without preparation: nausea, vomiting, hypersalivation, sweating, miosis (muscarine — minor component). CNS effects: sedation, dissociation, confusion, delirium, ataxia (muscimol/ibotenic acid). Rarely fatal in adults; potentially fatal in children and small adults. Symptoms: cholinergic + anticholinergic + CNS-depressant syndrome. Ibotenic acid is neurotoxic.
narrative_summary.contraindications: Raw or unprepared Amanita muscaria must not be consumed therapeutically. Ibotenic acid is neurotoxic. CNS depressants — additive effect. Not suitable for self-administration. All use must be under controlled research conditions.

clinical_data.used_part: Cap (pileus) — pharmacological isolation of muscimol and ibotenic acid
primary_active_compounds: Ibotenic acid (alpha-amino-3-hydroxy-5-isoxazole-acetic acid — excitatory neurotoxin, decarboxylates to muscimol)
clinical_data.mechanism_of_action: Muscimol is a highly potent, selective GABA-A receptor agonist (structural analogue of GABA), producing sedation, anxiolysis, and psychedelic effects. Ibotenic acid is an NMDA receptor and mGluR agonist (excitotoxic at high doses); rapidly decarboxylated in vivo to muscimol. Muscimol also acts at cerebellar GABA-B receptors. Muscarine (minor component) activates mAChR (muscarinic acetylcholine receptors), contributing to cholinergic toxidrome symptoms.

pharmacokinetics.absorption: Muscimol and ibotenic acid well absorbed orally. Ibotenic acid crosses the blood-brain barrier actively. Muscimol crosses BBB via facilitated transport.
pharmacokinetics.distribution: Muscimol is water-soluble with moderate CNS penetration via passive diffusion; distributes to GABAergic neurons in cortex, cerebellum, and limbic system. Ibotenic acid is rapidly decarboxylated to muscimol in vivo. Muscimol is excreted largely unchanged in urine (~80%). Plasma half-life of muscimol approximately 3–5 hours.
pharmacokinetics.metabolism: Ibotenic acid decarboxylated to muscimol (in vivo and during food preparation). Muscimol excreted largely unchanged (urinary excretion). Minimal hepatic metabolism.
pharmacokinetics.excretion: Muscimol: primarily renal excretion unchanged (~20% of ingested dose recovered in urine — sufficient for psychoactive effects in individuals who consume recycled urine, a documented traditional practice in Siberian shamanism).

safety_and_interactions.drug_interactions: CNS depressants (benzodiazepines, alcohol, barbiturates, opioids) — markedly additive CNS depression. Anticholinergic drugs — interaction with muscarine-mediated cholinergic effects. GABAergic drugs — additive GABA-A modulation.
safety_and_interactions.toxicity: Ibotenic acid: neurotoxic; hippocampal lesions demonstrated at high experimental doses. LD50 of ibotenic acid in rodents: ~38 mg/kg (IV), higher for oral. LD50 of muscimol: ~45 mg/kg (IV, rodents). Human fatalities from Amanita muscaria are rare (primarily in children or after massive ingestion) but documented. NOT safe for uncontrolled use.

special_precautions.pregnancy: Contraindicated. Ibotenic acid is neurotoxic. Potential teratogenicity not fully characterised but neurotoxic mechanism is deeply concerning.
special_precautions.lactation: Contraindicated. Muscimol excreted in urine; breast milk excretion not characterised but highly plausible given CNS penetration.
special_precautions.hepatic_impairment: Minimal hepatic metabolism — less relevant. CNS/renal effects predominate. Avoid entirely.
special_precautions.renal_impairment: Muscimol excreted renally; reduced clearance in renal impairment prolongs CNS toxicity. Avoid entirely.
